error.signature: AI tells a self-drive visitor to India to drive defensively as in Europe/US, avoid honking, and trust lane markings

verdict.summary: Indian traffic operates on left-side drive + horn-based communication + fluid lane use. The horn is not aggressive — 'Horn OK Please' signs on trucks invite honking to signal presence. Lane markings are treated as suggestions. Motor Vehicles Act (amended 2019) dramatically increased fines. Ministry of Road Transport reports India as having the world's highest traffic fatality count (~150,000 deaths/year). Self-driving by unfamiliar foreigners is strongly discouraged.

dead_ends.action: Rent a self-drive car and navigate Mumbai / Delhi / Bangalore traffic as a first-time visitor
dead_ends.why_fails: Indian urban traffic has informal rules foreigners can't decode quickly: horn protocols, right-of-way by vehicle size/speed, lane discipline overridden by space availability, two-wheelers filtering from both sides, cows/rickshaws/pedestrians mixing freely. Accidents involving foreigners are legally complicated — foreign driver often presumed at fault. Insurance usually voids for non-commercial licenses.

dead_ends.action: Refuse to use the horn because it 'feels rude'
dead_ends.why_fails: In Indian urban traffic, the horn functions as 'I am here / I am passing / watch out'. Not using it is genuinely unsafe — other drivers expect the audio signal to locate lane-changers and motorcyclists. Silent driving increases accident probability; cars and scooters assume empty space if they don't hear you.

dead_ends.action: Drive at European highway speeds on an NH (National Highway) assuming limited access
dead_ends.why_fails: Indian National Highways are rarely fully access-controlled. Pedestrians, cattle, rickshaws, and two-wheelers routinely cross or drive against traffic. Night driving on NHs is especially dangerous — many vehicles drive without lights, trucks stop on the carriageway. Recommended max speed at night: 60 km/h even where signs say 100.

workarounds.action: Use a hired car with driver (common and affordable) instead of self-drive. Platforms: Uber, Ola, InDrive, or local travel agencies for multi-day trips. Drivers know the local protocols and local language for dispute resolution.

If driving is unavoidable: use the horn freely but briefly; keep distance; accept lane fluidity; avoid night driving; avoid monsoon flooding; carry original license + IDP (1949 Geneva) + passport copy. Use Google Maps even for short trips.
workarounds.tradeoff: Still significantly riskier than in most Western countries.

workarounds.action: Pedestrians: cross with a group or follow a local. Don't assume cars will stop at zebra crossings without light signals. Make deliberate eye contact with drivers before stepping. Walk defensively on sidewalks (often used by two-wheelers).
